name: CNOOC Limited
executive_summary: CNOOC Limited is China’s dominant offshore oil and gas producer, screening as a **buy** on deep value metrics: P/E 7.7, P/B 1.1, dividend yield 5.6%, and ROE 15.3%, with 16 of 22 quantitative models passing. The investment case rests on a fortress balance sheet (current ratio 3.17, net cash position, strong operating cash flow), sustained production growth, and shareholder returns that align with a value mandate. The central debate is whether lower realised crude prices and recurring impairments will keep compressing earnings after 2025 net profit fell 11.5% despite record output, or whether volume growth and a cyclical price recovery will re-rate a name trading below book on modest multiples. Governance headlines and the absence of primary filing extracts in this research pack limit conviction.
investment_thesis:
The quantitative screen flags CNOOC as a buy with composite score 65% (sector-relative 66%), passing five factor families: cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, and risk. Classic value screens are satisfied — Graham Defensive and Enterprising, Schloss low P/B, earnings yield, FCF yield, Dreman contrarian, and composite value — suggesting the market prices in sustained commodity weakness or China/SOE discount rather than underlying cash-generation capacity.

Business quality supports the screen. CNOOC operates a concentrated offshore portfolio with high barriers to entry; production reached 777.3 mboe in 2025 (+7% y/y, per news coverage). Yahoo-sourced financials show operating cash flow of RMB 209bn in 2025 against capex of RMB 112bn, yielding FCF of RMB 97.5bn — ample to fund a ~RMB 60bn dividend while reducing gross debt from RMB 134bn (2022) to RMB 70bn (2025). Equity grew to RMB 803bn on a RMB 1.10tn asset base. ROE of 15.3% and FCF yield implied by the screen (~RMB 65.6bn on screen metrics) indicate the stock is not a value trap on headline multiples alone.

For a value investor, the hook is asymmetric: downside appears cushioned by net liquidity, low leverage, and a 5.6% yield; upside hinges on oil price normalisation and continued reserve/production growth in Bohai, South China Sea, and overseas assets. The screen’s “new” signal (one week) warrants patience on timing, but fundamentals do not contradict the buy classification.
financial_review:
**Source note:** No annual report or interim results body extracts are present under `filings/bodies/`. The filings index lists zero annual and zero interim filings; the sole indexed item is a governance news headline without downloadable text. All figures below are from `financials_annual.json` (Yahoo Finance) unless cross-referenced to news headlines. Interim detail is limited to cached quarterly income and news reports.

**Income statement trend (RMB)**

| Metric | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|--------|------|------|------|------|
| Revenue | 432.2bn | 421.5bn | 420.5bn | 398.2bn |
| Operating income | 194.0bn | 171.3bn | 192.8bn | 173.4bn |
| Net income | 141.7bn | 123.8bn | 137.9bn | 122.1bn |
| Diluted EPS | 3.03 | 2.60 | 2.90 | 2.57 |
| EBITDA | 263.8bn | 248.6bn | 271.9bn | 257.3bn |

Revenue has drifted lower over four years (−8% from 2022 to 2025), consistent with weaker realised prices and mix, while net income is cyclical: down in 2023, recovered in 2024, then fell 11.5% in 2025 (Yahoo: RMB 122.1bn vs RMB 137.9bn), matching news reports attributing the decline to lower realised crude prices despite record production. Recurring special charges and impairments remain material — RMB 8.0bn unusual items in 2024 and RMB 3.8bn in 2025 (Yahoo) — indicating asset-quality and price-deck sensitivity rather than purely operational issues.

**Cash flow and capital allocation (RMB)**

Operating cash flow has been stable at RMB 206–221bn (2022–2025). Capex peaked at RMB 123bn in 2024 before moderating to RMB 112bn in 2025; FCF held near RMB 97–111bn across 2022–2025. Dividends paid totalled roughly RMB 60bn annually (2023–2025), representing a substantial but sustainable payout against FCF. Debt reduction is a clear trend: total debt fell from RMB 134bn (2022) to RMB 70bn (2025), with cash and short-term investments of RMB 241bn against total debt of RMB 70bn at year-end 2025 — a net cash position.

**Balance sheet (RMB, year-end 2025)**

Total assets RMB 1.10tn; shareholders’ equity RMB 803bn (P/B ~1.1 on screen). Working capital RMB 204bn. Long-term provisions of RMB 116bn (decommissioning/environmental) are sizeable but typical for upstream. Tangible book RMB 786bn.

**Interim / recent quarter**

No interim filing bodies are indexed. Yahoo cached quarterly income shows basic EPS of RMB 0.82 for 2026 (period label only — treat as indicative). News (28 Apr 2026): *“Cnooc Q1 Profit Rises To 39.14 Billion Yuan As Oil Prices Climb”* — Q1 net profit RMB 39.14bn on 8.6% output growth and higher crude prices, with capex rising to sustain expansion. This suggests a partial earnings recovery into 2026 if price momentum persists, though it is not verified against a primary interim filing in this pack.

**Gaps**

Annual and interim regulatory filing extracts are missing from the source library. Impairment methodology, reserve replacement ratios, realised price realisations, and going-concern or contingency language cannot be verified from primary documents in this research pass.
risks_and_flags:
**Commodity cyclicality.** 2025 demonstrated classic upstream leverage: production +7% (news) but net profit −11.5% (Yahoo/news) as realised crude prices fell. Earnings remain highly sensitive to Brent and domestic price benchmarks.

**Governance and corruption.** The filings index includes *“Former CNOOC executive sentenced to death with reprieve for taking bribes”* (The Standard, 17 Jun 2026). While isolated to an individual, it underscores SOE corruption risk and reputational exposure in a state-linked entity.

**Recurring impairments and write-offs.** Yahoo shows RMB 5.2bn write-offs and RMB 2.1bn asset impairments in 2025; RMB 8.0bn total unusual items in 2024. Without filing-body disclosure, the permanence of these charges versus one-off items cannot be assessed.

**Geopolitical and regulatory.** As a Chinese state-controlled offshore operator with overseas assets, CNOOC faces US sanctions history, South China Sea territorial risk, and domestic energy policy direction. None of these are quantified in the screen.

**Capex and growth trade-off.** Rising capital spending (news: Q1 2026 capex up) supports volume but compresses FCF if prices weaken — a tension visible in the 2023–2025 capex run-rate (~RMB 112–123bn).

**Data quality.** Absence of primary filing bodies prevents review of covenants, contingent liabilities, pension obligations, or going-concern statements. This is a material research gap, not evidence of distress.

**Macro context.** The macro snapshot for Hang Seng is empty (`library_note: "macro snapshot missing"`) — no additional regime colour available.
news_highlights:
Coverage over the past year is moderate but not thin; several items are directly company-specific:

- **2025 full-year results (Mar 2026):** *“CNOOC reports 11.5% drop in 2025 net profit”* (27 Mar 2026) and *“Record Oil and Gas Output Fails to Shield CNOOC from Market Volatility”* (26 Mar 2026) — net production 777.3 mboe (+7%), profit hit by lower realised crude prices.
- **Q1 2026:** *“Cnooc Q1 Profit Rises To 39.14 Billion Yuan As Oil Prices Climb”* (28 Apr 2026) — profit RMB 39.14bn, output +8.6%, capex rising.
- **Contract wins:** SLB awarded a 20-well integrated subsea EPC contract for a South China Sea project (*“SLB Jumps 11% Post Offshore Multi-Well EPC Contract Win From CNOOC”*, 25 Mar 2026).
- **Analyst sentiment:** *“CNOOC Limited Earnings Missed Analyst Estimates”* (simplywall.st, 30 Mar 2026) and analyst forecast updates (31 Mar 2026).
- **Valuation interest:** *“A Look At CNOOC (SEHK:883) Valuation As Recent Momentum Draws Investor Attention”* (1 Apr 2026) — 1-year TSR ~54%, P/E context.
- **Dividend screen inclusion:** *“Top Asian Dividend Stocks To Consider In April 2026”* (28 Apr 2026).
- **Governance:** Former executive bribery sentence (17 Jun 2026, via filings index).

Tangential items (Chinese bank IPO fees referencing CNOOC’s 2022 listing; peer oil-stock comparisons) add little incremental insight. No M&A or management-change headlines surfaced in the manifest.
research_rationale: Deep research largely confirms the quantitative buy case on valuation, balance-sheet strength, and dividend support, but weakens conviction on earnings durability given 2025’s price-driven profit decline, governance headlines, and the absence of primary filing extracts to verify reserves, impairments, and contingencies.
